CAVALLINI COSTA RICA RESTORATIVE DENTAL TRAVEL CONTROL SYSTEM Chapter 5 — Procedure-Day Identity Verification, Sedation Control,…
Worksheet TWO COLUMN → Cavallini Procedure Execution and In-Country Recovery Stabilization

Onsite Pharmacy Medication and Post-Op Instruction Logging Sheet

Creates the patient-and-escort medication execution log by converting Cavallini discharge instructions into exact first-24-hour actions and urgent symptom thresholds.

⏱ 20 minutes at discharge and 10 minutes for first-night updates ↗ See also: IV Sedation Consent, Escort Presence, and Discharge Readiness Checklist · Procedure-Day Treatment Scope and Material Verification Record at Cavallini · Cavallini Discharge Packet Receipt and Missing-Document Resolution Form
Clinic-Given Medication or Restriction Instruction
Patient/Escort Execution Plan for the First 24 Hours in San José
Medication Reconciliation Between Cavallini Discharge Instructions and Pharmacy Labels
Before you start

Before completing this section, gather the printed prescriptions or medication list, the actual onsite pharmacy labels, and any nurse or dentist discharge sheet. This section should only be completed after ws-05-b has confirmed whether any first doses were already administered onsite. Compare each medication name, strength, and intended purpose line by line instead of relying on memory. This section is complete when every medication in hand has a matched instruction source and any mismatch has been escalated before leaving the clinic area.

Left-column entry: Antibiotic exactly as written on Cavallini prescription or pharmacy label *
Right-column execution: First antibiotic dose status after today’s procedure *
Left-column entry: Primary pain medication exactly as dispensed by onsite pharmacy *
Right-column execution: Pain-medication interval converted into clock times for tonight and overnight *
Food, Oral Hygiene, and Activity Restrictions After Implant, Extraction, Crown, or Full-Arch Work
Before you start

Before completing this section, review the written post-op instructions from Cavallini and ask staff to clarify any verbal-only restrictions involving rinsing, spitting, brushing, smoking, alcohol, prosthesis removal, or chewing. Use the actual procedure performed in ws-05-a, because restrictions differ if the patient had implants, grafting, full-arch temporaries, or a simpler crown visit. Do not summarize broadly; write what the patient may do and when. This section is complete when the first-night eating, cleaning, and activity rules are specific enough that the escort could enforce them without asking the clinic again.

Left-column entry: Eating and drinking restriction stated by Cavallini for the first 12 to 24 hours *
Right-column execution: First post-op meal plan at San José lodging *
Left-column entry: Oral hygiene and rinsing restriction given after today’s procedure *
Right-column execution: Bathroom-counter setup and hygiene timing for first night *
Urgent Symptom Thresholds and Contact Activation Rules
Before you start

Before completing this section, review the written symptom thresholds, urgent contact card, and any verbal instructions provided by the dentist, nurse, or pharmacy handoff. This section depends on the urgent contact route already being captured in ws-05-a or ws-05-e. Record thresholds in observable terms such as repeated gauze saturation, fever, worsening swelling, uncontrolled pain, or inability to maintain oral intake. This section is complete when the patient and escort know exactly what symptom triggers a message or call and what information must be sent to the clinic.

Left-column entry: Bleeding, swelling, fever, or pain threshold that requires urgent contact with Cavallini *
Right-column execution: What the escort will send or report if an urgent symptom occurs *
Left-column entry: After-hours contact route provided by Cavallini for tonight *
Right-column execution: Escort acknowledgement that urgent-contact route has been tested or saved correctly *
First-Night Dosing and Recovery Feasibility Check
Before you start

Before completing this section, use the medication names and restrictions already entered above, then compare them against transport time to lodging, sedation level, nausea risk, and sleep expectations. This section should be completed with the escort present if sedation or major surgery occurred. Convert every critical medication into realistic clock times and note any interval that seems hard to execute overnight. This section is complete when the first 24-hour plan is feasible without guessing and any unworkable schedule has been clarified with Cavallini before departure.

Left-column entry: Medication or recovery instruction most likely to be missed overnight *
Right-column execution: Corrected overnight plan approved by patient, escort, or Cavallini staff *
Right-column completion status: First-24-hour medication and restriction plan is executable at current lodging *
! Common Errors to Avoid
  • Copying the pharmacy bag label without reconciling whether the first dose was already given in the clinic chair area or recovery area.
  • Recording 'as needed' pain instructions without a defined severity threshold or maximum interval, which causes under-treatment or unsafe overuse in the hotel.
  • Failing to translate hygiene and food restrictions into clock-based actions for the first evening after sedation, especially when the patient is sleepy, nauseated, or traveling between clinic and lodging.
Decision Gates
Decision Gate — g-01 BLOCKS COMPLETION
Written Medication Record Gate
If the clinic cannot provide a written record of what was prescribed, dispensed, or changed that day.
Pass → Proceed
Complete ws-05-d using the written sources and verify label-to-instruction consistency before transport.
Fail → Remediate
Classify discharge as incomplete and remain onsite until documents are produced or a documented delivery deadline within 24 hours is assigned.
Decision Gate — g-02 BLOCKS COMPLETION
First-24-Hour Dosing Feasibility Gate
If the medication interval schedule cannot realistically be executed because of travel time, sleep, nausea, or patient impairment.
Pass → Proceed
Record the executable clock schedule and proceed to lodging with alarms and escort plan in place.
Fail → Remediate
Request Cavallini clarification on how to restart or adjust dosing, then update ws-05-d before departure.
Date Completed
Reviewed By
Repeat-use worksheet · Photocopy as needed
Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System 45
CAVALLINI COSTA RICA RESTORATIVE DENTAL TRAVEL CONTROL SYSTEM Chapter 5 — Procedure-Day Identity Verification, Sedation Control,…
Worksheet → Cavallini Procedure Execution and In-Country Recovery Stabilization

Cavallini Discharge Packet Receipt and Missing-Document Resolution Form

Confirms that the patient physically received the records, instructions, contacts, and follow-up commitments required to close the Cavallini visit and hand off safely to recovery and U.S. follow-up.

⏱ 15 minutes at discharge desk and 10 minutes if any record is missing ↗ See also: Procedure-Day Treatment Scope and Material Verification Record at Cavallini · Implant System, Abutment, and Prosthetic Specification Capture Form · Onsite Pharmacy Medication and Post-Op Instruction Logging Sheet · IV Sedation Consent, Escort Presence, and Discharge Readiness Checklist
Required Discharge Packet Receipt Audit
s-01
Before you start

Before completing this section, gather every paper, printout, receipt, medication sheet, implant sticker page, appointment card, and digital file received from Cavallini on procedure day. This section depends on ws-05-a and ws-05-d already identifying the actual procedure performed and the medications prescribed. Check each expected document physically rather than assuming it is somewhere in the packet. This section is complete when each required item is marked received, missing, or promised with a named delivery path.

Written post-operative instruction sheet physically received before leaving clinic * boolean
Mark true only if a written instruction sheet is actually in hand or saved digitally before departure.
Procedure summary describing what Cavallini actually performed today * boolean
Mark true only if the completed procedure is documented in writing, not just explained verbally.
Implant/prosthetic specification record, stickers, or lab documentation received if applicable * boolean
Mark true only if site/component information or source documents were physically received or emailed while onsite.
Medication list or prescriptions received with dosing instructions * boolean
Mark true only if medication names and dosing instructions are in writing.
Urgent after-hours contact route card, number, or WhatsApp instruction received * boolean
Mark true only if a direct usable route was provided in writing or saved digitally.
Next in-country follow-up appointment timing or review plan documented * boolean
Mark true only if date/time or a documented callback plan is present.
Missing-Document Escalation and 24-Hour Resolution Control
s-02
Before you start

Before completing this section, review any missing items from the receipt audit above and identify whether they are absent because of clinic delay, lab delay, or provider completion delay. Use the discharge desk, treatment coordinator, or named clinical staff member as the source for promised delivery details. Do not accept 'we will send it later' without a direct owner and timeframe. This section is complete when every missing item has a named responsible person, delivery channel, and deadline within 24 hours or a written explanation for why that is impossible.

Exact discharge item still missing at time patient is ready to leave Cavallini *
Implant sticker sheet and written confirmation whether delivered maxillary bridge is provisional conversion or final prosthesis
List every missing document specifically; enter 'None' only if packet is complete.
Named Cavallini staff member responsible for sending missing item *
Laura Gómez, international patient coordinator
Enter a real person’s name and role, not 'front desk'.
Promised delivery channel for missing discharge item * select
Email PDF to patient and escort
Select: Printed before patient leaves / Email PDF to patient / Email PDF to patient and escort / WhatsApp document send / Patient portal upload / REQUIRES CLARIFICATION
Choose the specific agreed channel for delivery.
Promised deadline for missing-item delivery *
By 3:00 PM tomorrow, 2026-07-23
Enter a specific date and time, not 'tomorrow' or 'soon'.
Escalation route if the missing item is not received by deadline *
Follow up with international coordinator first, then treating dentist office line and urgent WhatsApp if travel departure is within 12 hours
State a concrete escalation sequence with contacts or roles.
Warranty and Delivered-Status Clarification Capture
s-03
Before you start

Before completing this section, ask whether any warranty language discussed today applies to the actual work delivered, especially if a temporary, provisional, conversion prosthesis, or staged implant case was involved. Review any written warranty summary already obtained during due diligence and compare it against the procedure actually performed in ws-05-a and specifications captured in ws-05-c. Do not assume that a lifetime implant warranty covers provisional prosthetics, bite changes, fractures, or return-travel costs. This section is complete when the patient knows whether today’s delivered work is provisional or final and what treatment-specific warranty conditions were stated.

Delivered restoration status clarified at discharge * select
Provisional temporary only
Select: No restoration delivered / Provisional temporary only / Final restoration delivered / Mixed case — some provisional, some final / REQUIRES CLARIFICATION
Choose the actual status of what the patient left with today.
Treatment-specific warranty conditions stated for the work completed today *
Implant fixtures covered per clinic program, but maxillary conversion provisional not treated as final prosthesis; maintenance compliance and return evaluation required for warranty review.
Write the conditions tied to today's delivered work, not general website warranty language.
Return-to-Costa-Rica obligation or exclusion mentioned for warranty claims *
Any definitive remake or implant-related warranty review requires in-person evaluation at Cavallini; travel costs not stated as covered.
Enter exact travel or exclusion language, or 'REQUIRES CLARIFICATION' if not provided.
Duplication, Export, and Chapter Closure Confirmation
s-04
Before you start

Before completing this section, photograph or scan the entire discharge packet, completed worksheets, medication labels, and any implant stickers or lab documents. This section should be completed before leaving the clinic area or immediately on arrival at the onsite hotel if the clinic is still reachable for same-day correction. Verify that the recovery handoff and U.S. follow-up handoff each have usable source documents. This section is complete when backup copies exist, the packet has a destination for U.S. transfer, and chapter closure status can be truthfully marked complete or incomplete.

Discharge packet duplicated by photo, scan, or PDF before departure from clinic area * boolean
Mark true only if backup copies of key records were created the same day.
Location where duplicate packet is stored for access during Costa Rica recovery and after U.S. return *
iPhone Files folder 'Cavallini July 2026' plus emailed PDF copy to spouse and Dropbox medical folder
Name the actual storage locations, not just 'phone'.
U.S. recipient or future provider designated to receive discharge packet after return home *
Dr. Lauren Chen DDS secure email via office records coordinator after first post-return visit
Identify the U.S. recipient or enter 'REQUIRES CLARIFICATION' if not yet established.
Chapter-5 discharge handoff status at the moment patient leaves Cavallini * select
Operationally complete
Select: Operationally complete / Incomplete — missing documents pending / At-risk — no clear U.S. handoff destination / Escalated — unresolved same-day issue / REQUIRES CLARIFICATION
Choose 'Incomplete' if any required packet element, urgent contact route, or missing-item deadline is unresolved.
! Common Errors to Avoid
  • Leaving with only verbal instructions because the front desk promises to email documents later, but without naming who will send them or by when.
  • Failing to request treatment-specific warranty conditions when a provisional prosthesis was delivered, leading the patient to assume lifetime coverage applies immediately and fully.
  • Not duplicating the discharge packet before leaving the clinic or onsite hotel, which creates a single point of failure if papers are lost during return travel to the U.S.
Decision Gates
Decision Gate — g-01 BLOCKS COMPLETION
Written Discharge Packet Completeness Gate
If the clinic cannot provide a written record of what was placed, prescribed, or changed that day.
Pass → Proceed
Mark the packet complete, duplicate it, and release the case to recovery and U.S. handoff workflows.
Fail → Remediate
Classify discharge as incomplete and remain onsite until the documents are produced or a documented follow-up delivery deadline is assigned within 24 hours.
Decision Gate — g-02 BLOCKS COMPLETION
Urgent Contact and U.S. Handoff Readiness Gate
If the patient cannot identify a functioning urgent contact route or cannot identify who will receive U.S.-side records after return.
Pass → Proceed
Close Chapter 5 and hand off to recovery and long-term follow-up control.
Fail → Remediate
Flag the next domain as at-risk and treat the unresolved item as a priority task within 24 hours.
Date Completed
Reviewed By
Repeat-use worksheet · Photocopy as needed
Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System 46
06
Chapter 06

US Reentry Continuity of Care and Cavallini Warranty Preservation

Post-Operative Symptom Surveillance, Medication Adherence, Emergency Escalation, and Return-Flight Clearance in Costa Rica

Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System 4 control domains
Chapter 6 | US Reentry Continuity of Care and Cavallini Warranty Preservation

Post-Operative Symptom Surveillance, Medication Adherence, Emergency Escalation, and Return-Flight Clearance in Costa Rica

What this section is for
This chapter controls the recovery period in San José after Cavallini treatment so you can detect complications early, escalate correctly, and leave Costa Rica only when both clinical stability and records completeness are confirmed.
When it matters
It starts as soon as Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management gives you the discharge packet, medications, and post-op instructions, and it remains active until return-flight fitness and final packet completeness are both verified.
Failure looks like
The patient assumes worsening pain, swelling, bleeding, fever, nausea, or prosthetic problems are normal, does not log the trend, reaches flight day unstable, and either misses travel, boards without safe clearance, or returns to the U.S. without usable records for follow-up.
This chapter produces
  • Completed Costa Rica recovery symptom and medication log through departure decision point
  • Documented red-flag escalation record showing self-monitoring, clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency routing decisions
  • Return-flight fitness determination based on pain control, bleeding stability, swelling trend, hydration, mobility, and medication continuity
  • Duplicated final return packet for U.S. follow-up and warranty preservation
Minimum Viable Actions (Start Here)
Action 1
Confirm the procedure performed, sedation or anesthesia end time, medication list, and urgent clinic contact route before the first night begins.
Action 2
Start ws-06-a within 2 hours of arrival at recovery lodging and log pain, bleeding, swelling, temperature if relevant, oral intake, hydration, and every medication dose actually taken.
Action 3
Use ws-06-b at each check-in to classify symptoms into self-monitoring, same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency intervention.
Action 4
For implant placement, extractions, sinus work, or All-on-X/full-mouth treatment, run symptom checks at minimum morning, mid-day, evening, and bedtime for the first 48 hours.
Action 5
Before sleep on post-op day 0 and day 1, verify bleeding level, pain control, hydration, urination, medication completion, overnight contact ability, and safe mobility to the bathroom.
Action 6
Do not begin airport or flight-prep execution until ws-06-d supports commercial travel and the final return packet has been audited and duplicated.
Orientation Snapshot

Orientation Snapshot: This domain begins the moment Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management hands you the discharge packet, medications, and immediate post-procedure instructions. Its job is not comfort optimization in the abstract. Its job is to determine whether your post-operative course in San José is trending toward stable recovery, clinic reassessment, or emergency escalation before commercial travel is attempted.

The central operational risk is misclassifying a complication as "normal post-op discomfort" until the scheduled return flight is already imminent. That failure is expensive and unstable in cross-border dentistry. If an implant site is bleeding beyond expectation, a provisional full-arch prosthesis becomes functionally unusable, or swelling rises instead of plateaus, the consequence is not just one bad recovery day; it can mean extra hotel nights, rebooking flights, replacement medication, emergency transport, or a return to the U.S. without a usable handoff packet.

This chapter therefore converts recovery into a monitored control period with four outputs: a completed daily symptom and medication log, a documented red-flag escalation decision, a return-flight fitness determination, and a complete final return packet. If any one of those outputs is missing, this domain is not complete. The chapter closes only when you are both clinically stable for travel and administratively equipped for U.S. follow-up.

Decision rule: if the discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, or procedure details are missing on procedure day, then recovery monitoring cannot begin in a controlled way and you must obtain those items from Cavallini before treating the recovery stay as active. Downstream effect: Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment cannot be validated, and U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control will start with incomplet

…

Domain Purpose
Prevents the common dental tourism failure in which the patient returns home without a maintenance dentist, complication pathway, or documentation needed to preserve warranty rights.
Quick Reference Rules
→ Do not treat recovery as controlled until discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details are in hand — Rationale: symptom interpretation is invalid without knowing what was done and what was prescribed. Trigger: procedure-day discharge. Risk: missed complication signals, invalid travel clearance, and incomplete U.S. handoff.
→ Track symptoms on a fixed cadence rather than by memory — Rationale: trend reversal matters more than any single pain or swelling report. Trigger: first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or full-mouth treatment. Risk: delayed recognition of infection, bleeding, prosthetic instability, or medication failure.
→ Record every dose actually taken, including missed, delayed, or vomited doses — Rationale: worsening symptoms cannot be interpreted correctly while medication adherence is unclear. Trigger: every antibiotic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, or rinse event. Risk: reassessment delays, wrong clinical assumptions, and uncontrolled recovery drift.
→ If pain stays at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking medication as directed, contact Cavallini the same day — Rationale: rising pain despite adherence suggests a complication or inadequate control plan. Trigger: repeated high pain scores with documented adherence. Risk: lost in-country correction time before the booked return flight.
→ If active bleeding continues beyond the clinic's expected window, repeatedly soaks gauze, pools, or restarts after stopping, stop travel-prep tasks and escalate immediately — Rationale: ongoing bleed risk is incompatible with airport processing and flight stability. Trigger: persistent or recurrent active bleeding. Risk: emergency transport, flight cancellation, and unmanaged post-op instability.
→ Treat fever of 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, especially with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise, as an urgent reassessment path — Rationale: a red-flag cluster may indicate infection affecting healing or prosthetic stability. Trigger: fever plus worsening symptoms after early recovery. Risk: implant compromise, antibiotic changes under time pressure, and forced itinerary extension.
→ Do not self-clear for flight just because you feel better than yesterday — Rationale: commercial travel requires sustained function, hydration, medication continuity, mobility, and symptom predictability across a 6- to 10-hour travel block. Trigger: departure planning. Risk: instability during airport processing, missed flights, or post-arrival decompensation.
→ Do not leave San José without a duplicated return packet containing procedure summary, implant or prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, and written complication instructions — Rationale: U.S. dentists and warranty processes depend on exact documentation. Trigger: clinic checkout and airport departure preparation. Risk: refusal of follow-up care, warranty ambiguity, and duplicate imaging or remake costs.
Decision Guide
Can controlled recovery monitoring begin?
If: You have the discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details from Cavallini on procedure day.
Then: Open ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c and establish the baseline within 2 hours of arriving at lodging.
Else: Obtain the missing items from Cavallini before treating the recovery stay as operationally active.
Escalate when: Any required discharge item is still missing by the first monitoring interval or before the first night.
Is the patient staying in the self-monitoring lane?
If: Bleeding is light and decreasing, pain is controlled, fluids are tolerated, and the patient can ambulate safely.
Then: Continue the scheduled monitoring cadence and maintain medication adherence logging in ws-06-a.
Else: Reclassify the issue in ws-06-b to same-day clinic contact or higher.
Escalate when: Any monitored variable trends in the wrong direction for 2 consecutive check-ins.
Does pain require same-day clinic contact?
If: Pain remains at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking prescribed medication as directed.
Then: Contact Cavallini the same day with the logged timeline, medication record, and current function status.
Else: Continue self-monitoring if pain is improving or responds predictably to medication.
Escalate when: Pain is rising, waking the patient, or cannot be projected as manageable through the planned travel window.
Does bleeding require immediate escalation?
If: Active bleeding repeatedly soaks gauze, pools rather than diminishes, continues beyond the clinic's expected window, or restarts after having stopped.
Then: Pause all travel-prep tasks and obtain immediate clinic guidance using ws-06-c contact routes.
Else: Remain in self-monitoring if only light oozing or pink saliva is present and the trend is decreasing.
Escalate when: Pressure measures fail or bleeding is still active near departure day.
Is there an infection-pathway concern?
If: Temperature reaches 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, or fever appears with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise.
Then: Request urgent reassessment before any flight segment proceeds and document the symptom cluster in ws-06-a and ws-06-b.
Else: Continue trend monitoring if no fever cluster exists and symptoms are stable or improving.
Escalate when: The fever cluster appears after temporary improvement or is accompanied by worsening facial swelling or increasing pain.
Is medication adherence operationally intact?
If: Medications are being taken on schedule, kept down, and tolerated with adequate hydration or food as instructed.
Then: Continue the prescribed schedule and log every dose, including response and tolerance.
Else: Mark the event as missed, delayed by more than 2 hours, or vomited and contact Cavallini if adherence cannot be restored.
Escalate when: The patient cannot drink adequate fluids, keep medications down, or tolerate soft food for 12 hours or more after the expected sedation recovery period.
Is oral function and prosthetic stability acceptable?
If: The patient can swallow, sip water, take medications, tolerate the assigned diet stage, and close without a sudden high bite, clicking, or localized mechanical pain.
Then: Maintain the soft-diet restrictions and continue monitoring without stress-testing the restoration.
Else: Stop testing chewing function and request same-stay reassessment in San José.
Escalate when: A provisional feels unstable, a sudden high spot develops, repeated clicking occurs, or localized closure pain appears before departure.
Is the patient fit for commercial return travel?
If: Symptoms are stable or improving, bleeding is controlled, hydration and soft intake are adequate, mobility supports airport transit, medication continuity is intact, and ws-06-d is complete.
Then: Approve transition to departure execution and prepare the cross-domain handoff package for U.S. follow-up.
Else: Remain in Costa Rica recovery control and obtain Cavallini reassessment before self-clearing for flight.
Escalate when: The patient still requires rescue pain medication at intervals shorter than prescribed, cannot predict symptom control through a 6- to 10-hour travel block, or cannot move safely through transport transitions.
Is checkout administratively complete?
If: The return packet includes procedure summary, implant brand and components when applicable, prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, written post-op instructions, and complication guidance.
Then: Duplicate the packet before leaving San José and attach it to the downstream U.S. follow-up handoff.
Else: Stop departure prep and request the missing documents from Cavallini.
Escalate when: Any packet element needed for U.S. follow-up or warranty preservation is missing on airport day.
Trigger Blocks
  • Trigger: Arrival at hotel or recovery lodging after discharge -> Response: establish baseline in ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c within 2 hours.
  • Trigger: IV sedation or major surgery with companion present -> Response: assign the companion as primary recorder for the first monitoring interval and first night checks.
  • Trigger: Two consecutive worsening symptom check-ins -> Response: move the issue out of observation and into same-day clinic contact or urgent reassessment.
  • Trigger: Pain 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive intervals despite adherence -> Response: contact Cavallini the same day with the logged trend and medication record.
  • Trigger: Repeated gauze saturation, pooled bleeding, or recurrent bleeding after prior control -> Response: suspend travel preparation and seek immediate clinic guidance.
  • Trigger: Fever 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, especially with swelling, foul taste, or malaise -> Response: request urgent reassessment before any flight proceeds.
  • Trigger: Inability to keep fluids, soft food, or medications down for 12 hours or more after expected sedation recovery -> Response: contact Cavallini because symptom interpretation and healing support are no longer reliable.
  • Trigger: Sudden high bite, clicking, localized closure pain, or suspected provisional instability -> Response: stop chewing tests and request same-stay prosthetic reassessment.
  • Trigger: Planned departure day arrives -> Response: complete ws-06-d and audit the final return packet before any airport transfer.
Risk Blocks
  • Risk: Misclassifying a complication as normal post-op discomfort -> Mitigation: use scheduled trend logging and red-flag classification instead of memory or reassurance alone.
  • Risk: Medication failure hidden by poor documentation -> Mitigation: record every actual dose, delay, skipped dose, vomiting event, and tolerance problem in ws-06-a.
  • Risk: Escalation delay caused by missing phone numbers or unclear routing -> Mitigation: preload Cavallini daytime, after-hours, lodging, transport, and local emergency routes in ws-06-c before a problem develops.
  • Risk: Flight-day instability after unchecked bleeding, swelling, pain, or dehydration -> Mitigation: block travel-prep tasks until ws-06-d supports commercial travel and no unresolved red-flag remains.
  • Risk: Early infection compromising implants or full-mouth stability -> Mitigation: treat fever plus worsening swelling, pain, foul taste, or malaise as an urgent reassessment cluster rather than waiting for scheduled follow-up.
  • Risk: Provisional fracture or loosening from early chewing tests -> Mitigation: measure function narrowly, follow diet restrictions, and escalate mechanical symptoms before leaving San José.
  • Risk: Sedated patient treated as independently reliable on the first night -> Mitigation: require escort verification of swallowing, orientation, dose completion, and safe bathroom mobility when IV sedation was used.
  • Risk: U.S. follow-up failure due to missing implant brand, component, or prosthetic data -> Mitigation: audit and duplicate the return packet before airport departure.
Worksheet Linkage
Costa Rica Dental Recovery Daily Symptom and Medication Tracking Log
Use when: Use immediately after arrival at recovery lodging and continue through the departure decision point, with at least morning, mid-day, evening, and bedtime entries for the first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or full-mouth treatment.
Unblocks: It enables trend-based symptom interpretation, defensible clinic communication, and documented handoff to the U.S. follow-up domain.
Post-Implant and Full-Mouth Surgery Red-Flag Escalation Checklist for San José Stay
Use when: Use at every symptom check-in and whenever pain, swelling, bleeding, fever, nausea, oral function, or prosthetic stability changes.
Unblocks: It enables correct routing between self-monitoring, same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, and emergency intervention.
Clinic Contact, Local Emergency Option, and Escalation Routing Form for Post-Op Issues
Use when: Use within the first 2 hours of reaching lodging, before a problem occurs, to preload Cavallini daytime contacts, after-hours route if provided, onsite hotel assistance, transportation fallback, and local emergency options.
Unblocks: It enables rapid escalation without losing time searching for numbers, medication names, or transport support during a complication.
Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment
Use when: Use on departure day and earlier if symptom instability raises doubt about airport processing or a 6- to 10-hour travel block.
Unblocks: It enables defensible go/no-go travel clearance and proper transition into U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
Detailed Explanation

This domain converts post-op recovery from a vague waiting period into a controlled monitoring cycle. The operational objective is not to eliminate discomfort; it is to detect whether the recovery pattern is stabilizing, deteriorating, or becoming unsafe for travel while the patient is still inside Cavallini's correction window in San José. That distinction matters more in cross-border dental care than in local care because each lost day compresses the time available for reassessment before the booked return flight.

The chapter begins only after usable discharge inputs exist. Without the written instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details, there is no reliable baseline for deciding whether pain, bleeding, swelling, nausea, oral function limits, or temperature changes are expected versus abnormal. That is why the first action is administrative verification, followed immediately by ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c setup. If IV sedation or major surgery occurred, the companion is not optional support; during the first interval and first night, the companion is a control function because the patient may not be a reliable recorder.

Symptom surveillance is built around trends. A single pain score has weak value by itself. A pain curve rising from 4/10 to 8/10 despite documented medication adherence is materially different from stable soreness that drops after dosing. The same applies to swelling, bleeding, fever clusters, oral intake, and function. This is why the chapter requires scheduled check-ins, especially during the first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus-related work, or full-mouth treatment. The point is to catch deterioration early enough that San José reassessment is still feasible.

Medication adherence is treated as a control variable because broken adherence contaminates the symptom picture. If an antibiotic was skipped, an analgesic was delayed, or medication was vomited after dosing, the clinical interpretation changes. A worsening symptom pattern with intact adherence points toward one decision path; the same pattern with failed adherence may point toward another. Logging actual use rather than intended use allows Cavallini to respond to a clean record instead of a confused verbal summary.

Escalation logic in this chapter is based on progression, function, and control, not anxiety. Light decreasing oozing, tolerable pain, successful hydration, and safe ambulation support self-monitoring. Two consecutive worsening check-ins, persistent high pain despite medication, recurrent active bleeding, a fever-plus-swelling cluster, inability to keep medications down, or mechanical instability of a provisional move the patient into clinic contact or urgent reassessment. Airway-threatening symptoms or severe fun

…

Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System 48
Chapter 6 · Quick Reference
US Reentry Continuity of Care and Cavallini Warranty Preservation

Post-Operative Symptom Surveillance, Medication Adherence, Emergency Escalation, and Return-Flight Clearance in Costa Rica

Operating Rules
01 Do not treat recovery as controlled until discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details are in hand — Rationale: symptom interpretation is invalid without knowing what was done and what was prescribed. Trigger: procedure-day discharge. Risk: missed complication signals, invalid travel clearance, and incomplete U.S. handoff.
02 Track symptoms on a fixed cadence rather than by memory — Rationale: trend reversal matters more than any single pain or swelling report. Trigger: first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or full-mouth treatment. Risk: delayed recognition of infection, bleeding, prosthetic instability, or medication failure.
03 Record every dose actually taken, including missed, delayed, or vomited doses — Rationale: worsening symptoms cannot be interpreted correctly while medication adherence is unclear. Trigger: every antibiotic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, or rinse event. Risk: reassessment delays, wrong clinical assumptions, and uncontrolled recovery drift.
04 If pain stays at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking medication as directed, contact Cavallini the same day — Rationale: rising pain despite adherence suggests a complication or inadequate control plan. Trigger: repeated high pain scores with documented adherence. Risk: lost in-country correction time before the booked return flight.
05 If active bleeding continues beyond the clinic's expected window, repeatedly soaks gauze, pools, or restarts after stopping, stop travel-prep tasks and escalate immediately — Rationale: ongoing bleed risk is incompatible with airport processing and flight stability. Trigger: persistent or recurrent active bleeding. Risk: emergency transport, flight cancellation, and unmanaged post-op instability.
06 Treat fever of 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, especially with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise, as an urgent reassessment path — Rationale: a red-flag cluster may indicate infection affecting healing or prosthetic stability. Trigger: fever plus worsening symptoms after early recovery. Risk: implant compromise, antibiotic changes under time pressure, and forced itinerary extension.
07 Do not self-clear for flight just because you feel better than yesterday — Rationale: commercial travel requires sustained function, hydration, medication continuity, mobility, and symptom predictability across a 6- to 10-hour travel block. Trigger: departure planning. Risk: instability during airport processing, missed flights, or post-arrival decompensation.
08 Do not leave San José without a duplicated return packet containing procedure summary, implant or prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, and written complication instructions — Rationale: U.S. dentists and warranty processes depend on exact documentation. Trigger: clinic checkout and airport departure preparation. Risk: refusal of follow-up care, warranty ambiguity, and duplicate imaging or remake costs.
Worksheets
→ Costa Rica Dental Recovery Daily Symptom and Medication Tracking Log
→ Post-Implant and Full-Mouth Surgery Red-Flag Escalation Checklist for San José Stay
→ Clinic Contact, Local Emergency Option, and Escalation Routing Form for Post-Op Issues
→ Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment
Cascade Triggers

Downstream impacts if this chapter fails.

! When Cavallini discharge instructions, medication list, contact routes, and procedure details are received, trigger recovery monitoring execution in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.
! When any symptom exceeds the clinic's post-op threshold during the San José stay, trigger immediate reassessment or emergency escalation before any flight segment proceeds.
! When ws-06-d confirms commercial travel fitness and the final return packet is complete, trigger stateside handoff preparation in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
! When the patient leaves Costa Rica without unresolved red-flag issues and with duplicated records, trigger warranty-preserving follow-up scheduling and U.S. dentist transfer in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
Key Contacts
Role Name / Contact
Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System 49
CAVALLINI COSTA RICA RESTORATIVE DENTAL TRAVEL CONTROL SYSTEM Chapter 6 — Post-Operative Symptom Surveillance, Medication Adhere…
Worksheet TABLE → US Reentry Continuity of Care and Cavallini Warranty Preservation

Costa Rica Dental Recovery Daily Symptom and Medication Tracking Log

Produces the day-by-day recovery trend record needed to detect deterioration, document actual medication adherence, and support flight-clearance and U.S. handoff decisions after Cavallini restorative treatment.

⏱ 25 minutes on first use, 5-8 minutes per monitoring interval ↗ See also: Post-Implant and Full-Mouth Surgery Red-Flag Escalation Checklist for San José Stay · Clinic Contact, Local Emergency Option, and Escalation Routing Form for Post-Op Issues · Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment
Example entry
# Recovery Check-In Date and Local San José Time Post-Op Day Number and Monitoring Interval Exact Cavallini Procedure Being Recovered From Pain Score 0-10 and Response After Most Recent Medication Swelling Location / Symmetry / Effect on Swallowing or Mouth Opening Bleeding Status Since Last Check (oozing / pink saliva / active gauze soaking) Temperature °F, Oral Intake, Hydration, and Mobility Status Medication Dose Actually Taken or Missed Since Last Check-In Escalation Lane Selected From ws-06-b and Action Taken
1 2026-02-12 8:00 PM Day 0 — evening hotel arrival 4 extractions + 6 upper implants + IV sedation + immediate provisional full-arch 6/10 before analgesic; down to 3/10 within 45 min after prescribed dose Bilateral upper-jaw swelling starting; no swallowing difficulty; mouth opening mildly limited Light oozing; gauze changed once; no soaking 98.6 F; tolerated 8 oz water + yogurt; walked to bathroom with escort Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg at 7:30 PM taken with food; ibuprofen 600 mg at 7:45 PM; chlorhexidine not started yet per instructions Routine self-monitoring — continue scheduled checks
2 2026-02-13 2:00 PM Day 1 — mid-day 4 extractions + 6 upper implants + IV sedation + immediate provisional full-arch 7/10 despite taking analgesic at 11:30 AM; only improved to 6/10 Right-side facial swelling greater than left; tighter mouth opening; swallowing still possible No active gauze soaking; saliva lightly blood-tinged 100.5 F; sips of water only; nausea after soup; walking slowly but stable Antibiotic taken 2 hours late due to nausea; anti-inflammatory taken; afternoon pain medication taken on schedule Same-day clinic contact — message sent to Cavallini with fever + unilateral swelling timeline
# Recovery Check-In Date and Local San José Time Post-Op Day Number and Monitoring Interval Exact Cavallini Procedure Being Recovered From Pain Score 0-10 and Response After Most Recent Medication Swelling Location / Symmetry / Effect on Swallowing or Mouth Opening Bleeding Status Since Last Check (oozing / pink saliva / active gauze soaking) Temperature °F, Oral Intake, Hydration, and Mobility Status Medication Dose Actually Taken or Missed Since Last Check-In Escalation Lane Selected From ws-06-b and Action Taken
